title: TSMC CEO warns AI chip supply won't meet demand for years
subtitle: At the June 4 annual shareholder meeting, C.C. Wei said AI orders are outrunning capacity expansion even as the board approves $45 billion in new capex.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: TSMC held its 2026 annual shareholder meeting in Hsinchu on June 4, and Chairman and CEO C.C. Wei told reporters the company will not be able to satisfy AI-driven chip demand for years. 'Customer demand is so high, and we can only support so much. We are already working very hard,' Wei said. The board approved roughly $45 billion in capital appropriations covering advanced process capacity, advanced packaging, specialty technology, and new fab construction. Shareholders ratified 2025 results: NT$3.8 trillion in consolidated revenue and NT$1.7 trillion in net income.

The supply warning matters because TSMC is the single bottleneck in the AI hardware stack. Nvidia, AMD, Broadcom, and most hyperscaler custom silicon all route through TSMC's leading-edge nodes and CoWoS advanced packaging. If Wei is telling shareholders that capacity will lag demand for years, every roadmap downstream of TSMC — Nvidia Rubin, Google's TPU v8 ramp, AWS Trainium, hyperscaler buildouts — is implicitly constrained on the supply side, not the demand side, of the boom.

The $45 billion capex line is the most aggressive in TSMC's history and lands on top of the $52-56 billion 2026 plan disclosed in April. The math on TSMC's full-year guidance — more than 30% revenue growth in U.S. dollar terms — has held up through tariff turbulence, the China export-control regime, and the Trump administration's June 2 frontier-AI executive order. AI chip revenue is now projected to compound 50%+ annually through 2029 according to TSMC management.

A note for learners: the AI story is often told as a software story — models, agents, evaluations. The supply-side reality is that every capability you read about depends on whether one company in Taiwan can pour concrete and install EUV lithography fast enough. If you want to understand which AI bets will and won't materialize on schedule, learn to read foundry capacity and packaging constraints, not just model release notes.
